sales.title:
Ancients
SYRIA. Antioch. Augustus (27 BC-AD 14). AR tetradrachm (27mm, 15.23 gm, 1h). NGC AU 5/5 - 3/5, Fine Style. Dated Actian Era Year 30 and Consular Year 13 (2/1 BC). ΚΑΙΣΑΡΟΣ ΣΕ-ΒΑΣΤΟΥ, laureate head of Augustus right; bead-and-reel border / ΕΤΟΥΣ-Λ(date)-ΝΙΚΗΣ, Tyche of Antioch seated right on rocky outcropping, palm in outstretched right hand, half-length figure of river god Orontes swimming right below, head facing; ΠΑV monogram and IΓ over ΑΝΤ monogram in right field, dotted border. RPC I 4156. Gunmetal toning with a high-relief portrait. Born on 23 September 63 BC into a wealthy plebeian family, Augustus, originally Gaius Octavius, was the grandnephew and posthumously adopted son of Julius Caesar. This inheritance set him on a collision course with Rome's political elite, leading to the formation of the Second Triumvirate with Marc Antony and Marcus Aemilius Lepidus. Their initial unity against Caesar's assassins eventually gave way to conflict, from which Augustus emerged as Rome's first true emperor after victories in key battles like Philippi and Actium. In 27 BC, he was honored with the title "Augustus" by the Senate, signaling the start of his reign and the Pax Romana, a golden era of peace, prosperity, and cultural flourishing that lasted over two centuries. Augustus' rule saw significant military, administrative, and cultural reforms that stabilized and enriched the empire. He established a standing army, including the Praetorian Guard, reorganized Rome's provinces, and became a patron of the arts, fostering the careers of poets like Virgil and Horace and commissioning monumental buildings that reshaped Rome. His savvy use of coinage to communicate his achievements and vision helped solidify his power and legacy. Augustus died in AD 14, he left behind a transformed Rome. Through Suetonius's "The Twelve Caesars" and his own "Res Gestae Divi Augusti," we glimpse the life and achievements of a ruler who balanced autocracy with republican ideals, setting a precedent for future emperors and leaving an indelible mark on Roman history. HID05401242017 © 2025 Heritage Auctions | All Rights Reserved www.HA.com/TexasAuctioneerLicenseNotice

MACEDONIAN KINGDOM. Philip III Arrhidaeus (323-317 BC). AR drachm (17mm, 4.24 gm, 5h). NGC AU 5/5 - 3/5, brushed. Lifetime issue, Abydus, ca. 323-317 BC. Head of Heracles right, wearing lion skin headdress, paws tied before neck / ΦΙΛΙΠΠΟΥ, Zeus seated left on backless throne, right leg drawn back, feet on stool, eagle in outstretched right hand, grounded scepter in left; owl left in left field, horse leg below strut. Price P21. Arrhidaeus was the eldest son of Philip II and the half-brother of Alexander the Great. Despite being older than Alexander, Arrhidaeus was illegitimate: his mother a Thessalian woman outside of Philip II's marriage to Olympias. He also seemed to have mental deficiencies, what many modern scholars attribute to epilepsy. Thus, Alexander was deemed heir without contest. During Alexander's campaigns, Arrhidaeus stayed in Macedon under the watchful eye of Antipater, a general and father to Cassander. When Alexander died in 323 BC, Arrhidaeus was ultimately deemed heir as Roxana was still pregnant with Alexander's son - taking on the title Philip III Arrhidaeus. Soon after, he would marry Eurydice, an intelligent noblewoman who aimed to substantiate his rule as he was simply a figurehead to the regent Perdiccas. Yet, as the Wars of the Diadochi broke out in 322 BC, Perdiccas was killed and Antipater gained control of Arrhidaeus. He would appoint Polyperchon, an older officer, as his successor, which led Cassander, backed by Eurydice, to revolt. By 317 BC, Cassander was officially the regent to Arrhidaeus. In response, Olympias and her coalition invaded Macedon with Roxana's now young son, Alexander IV, in tow. As Alexander's biological son, Alexander IV was considered the lawful successor. With the army turning their loyalty to Alexander IV, Arrhidaeus and Eurydice were killed by Olympias. However, when Cassander returned from his campaign the following year, he had Olympias executed and would later assassinate Alexander IV - becoming king by 305 BC. HID05401242017 © 2025 Heritage Auctions | All Rights Reserved www.HA.com/TexasAuctioneerLicenseNotice

Magnus Maximus, Western Roman Empire (AD 383-388). AV solidus (20mm, 4.46 gm, 6h). NGC AU 5/5 - 3/5, scuff, brushed. London (Augusta), AD 384. D N MAG MA-XIMVS P F AVG, rosette-diademed, draped, and cuirassed bust of Magnus Maximus right, seen from front / VICTOR-IA AVGG, Magnus Maximus and Theodosius I seated facing on double throne, jointly holding globe between them; half length figure of Victory above facing between, vertical palm branch between their feet, AVGOB in exergue. RIC IX 2b. Depeyrot 2/1. Sharply struck from attractive style dies and excellently centered. Lovely, muted luster. Extremely rare.   HID05401242017  © 2025 Heritage Auctions | All Rights Reserved www.HA.com/TexasAuctioneerLicenseNotice

sales.title: Magnus Maximus, Western Roman Empire (AD 383-388). AV solidus (22mm, 4.42 gm, 1h). NGC MS 5/5 - 3/5. Trier, ca. AD 383-384. D N MAG MA-XIMVS P F AVG, rosette-diademed, draped, and cuirassed bust of Magnus Maximus right, seen from front / VICTOR-IA AVGG, Magnus Maximus and Theodosius I seated facing on double throne, jointly holding globe between them; half length figure of Victory above facing between, vertical palm branch between their feet, TROB in exergue. RIC IX 77b. A handsome portrait on a large flan.   A capable general of Spanish birth, Flavius Magnus Clemens Maximus was appointed military commander of Britain in AD 380. Three years later, Maximus made a bid for the throne of the Western Roman Empire, then ruled by the ineffectual Gratian and his young brother Valentinian II. To secure the loyalty of the British legions, Maximus reopened the old mint of Londinium, which had been closed for more than fifty years, and began striking gold solidi acclaiming himself as Augustus. After disposing of Gratian, Maximus proposed a three-way division of the Empire, with himself ruling Britain, Gaul, Germany and Spain, Valentinian II ruling Italy and Africa, and Theodosius I, another Spaniard, holding the East. This uneasy equilibrium lasted about three years but, thereafter, Maximus began encroaching upon Valentinian II's territory. The mintmark TR is augmented by the letters OB (standing for Obryzium, or "pure gold"), and the reverse type and legend (VICTORIA AVGG) explicitly acknowledge only two Roman emperors, surely Maximus and Theodosius I. In AD 387, Maximus raised his son Flavius Victor to the rank of Augustus and launched an invasion of Italy, provoking a final showdown with Theodosius. But in a lightning campaign, Theodosius struck eastward and defeated Maximus before he could fully martial his forces. Both Maximus and Victor were summarily executed by Theodosius, who restored Valentinian II to the Western throne.   HID05401242017  © 2025 Heritage Auctions | All Rights Reserved www.HA.com/TexasAuctioneerLicenseNotice
